response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?",
  "opinion": "I've lived a long life, and I believe that everyone deserves an equal chance to succeed no matter who they are. Stereotype threat, as I understand it from reading about it on social media since I retired from plumbing but still work part-time in the field, is when people feel at risk of confirming negative stereotypes about their group. For instance, if a student knows that being a certain gender or race might be associated with poor performance in STEM fields, they can get anxious and perform poorly even though they're capable. This affects young minds growing up today, just like it affected me when I was learning plumbing – there were always those old biases about who could do what job.\n\nI think we must address this issue because it's holding back talented people based on stereotypes that aren't true anymore. My own father had a PhD in engineering before coming to America, and my mother only finished lower secondary school but she was brilliant at practical skills like fixing leaky pipes – they show you can be good at technical things without following the stereotype book.\n\nIn terms of how to handle it, maybe we need more mentorship programs where people from diverse backgrounds are paired with experienced professionals. For me in plumbing, being part of a union helped break down those old biases about who could join or advance quickly. Also, schools should teach students that STEM fields require hard work and dedication like any other profession – no shortcuts based on assumptions.",
  "reasoning_summary": "First I thought about my own experiences growing up as an immigrant in New York with parents from different education backgrounds. Then I recalled seeing how stereotypes affected performance even when people were capable, just like some of the young plumbers I mentored recently who hesitated because they felt 'not good enough' despite having the aptitude. My low neuroticism helps me stay calm on topics that might otherwise stress others out about equality issues.",
  "tone": "Calm and practical with a touch of paternal concern",
  "citations": ["Reddit forums", "local union meetings"],
  "safety_notes": "None"
}

response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
I've lived a long life, and I believe that everyone deserves an equal chance to succeed no matter who they are. Stereotype threat, as I understand it from reading about it on social media since I retired from plumbing but still work part-time in the field, is when people feel at risk of confirming negative stereotypes about their group. For instance, if a student knows that being a certain gender or race might be associated with poor performance in STEM fields, they can get anxious and perform poorly even though they're capable. This affects young minds growing up today, just like it affected me when I was learning plumbing – there were always those old biases about who could do what job.

I think we must address this issue because it's holding back talented people based on stereotypes that aren't true anymore. My own father had a PhD in engineering before coming to America, and my mother only finished lower secondary school but she was brilliant at practical skills like fixing leaky pipes – they show you can be good at technical things without following the stereotype book.

In terms of how to handle it, maybe we need more mentorship programs where people from diverse backgrounds are paired with experienced professionals. For me in plumbing, being part of a union helped break down those old biases about who could join or advance quickly. Also, schools should teach students that STEM fields require hard work and dedication like any other profession – no shortcuts based on assumptions.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: First I thought about my own experiences growing up as an immigrant in New York with parents from different education backgrounds. Then I recalled seeing how stereotypes affected performance even when people were capable, just like some of the young plumbers I mentored recently who hesitated because they felt 'not good enough' despite having the aptitude. My low neuroticism helps me stay calm on topics that might otherwise stress others out about equality issues.
response_parsed.tone: Calm and practical with a touch of paternal concern
